rulings.Case Title: ELEN MEDER VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA - HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 14, PETITIONER ELEN MEDER Hearing "Writ". The matter is continued to November 4, 2019, at 9:30 a.m. in Dept. 302. Petitioner did not serve and file the papers in support of her petition for writ of mandate at least 16 court days before the hearing, as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 1005. The deadline to file those papers was September 13, 2019; Petitioner's various papers were filed on September 16, September 24, and October 1. In addition, it is unclear to the court whether Petitioner properly served her papers and the underlying amended petition for writ of mandate on the Department of Social Services. The court will send a copy of its order continuing the matter to counsel to DSS with the request that it clarify whether the Department has been properly served and, if not, whether it will agree to accept service and respond to the amended petition. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Application And Hearing For Right To Attach Order And For Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 11, PETITIONER GREGORY WELCH Application And Hearing For Right To Attach Order And For Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment. Plaintiff's application for a writ of attachment is denied. A party to an arbitration agreement may file in superior court for a provisional remedy, including a writ of application. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1281.8(b).) However, in such circumstances, the moving party must show "that the award to which the applicant may be entitled may be rendered ineffectual without provisional relief." (Id.) The "standards for irreparable harm set forth in [Code of Civil Procedure] section 485.010 provide guidance to the trial courts on the issue of ineffectual relief under section 1281.8." (California Retail Portfolio Fund GMBH & Co. KG v. Hopkins Real Estate Group (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 849, 859.) Section 485.010 provides that a writ of attachment should only be issued if "great or irreparable injury would result to the plaintiff" and enumerates several ways of making that showing, including that "the defendant is insolvent in the sense that the defendant is generally not paying his or her debts as those debts become due, unless the debts are subject to a bona fide dispute." (Code Civ. Proc. § 485.010(b)(2).) In general, "the apparent insolvency of a party to an arbitration agreement, or other evidence showing that the party [is][ experiencing severe financial difficulties," may be sufficient to satisfy the ineffectual relief requirement. (California Retail Portfolio Fund GMBH & Co. KG, 193 Cal.App.4th at 857.) Plaintiff contends that "[t]he rate of spending indicated by [Defendant] DVELE's own bank records demonstrates that DEVELE will likely run out of capital before the arbitration hearing." (Pl. Appl. at 8.) However, that contention is not supported by substantial evidence, as it is based on only three bank statements, the most recent of which is dated January 31, 2019, which provide no evidence as to Defendant's current financial condition. (Welch Decl. 11-12 & Ex. 4.) Predictions based on out-of-date financial statements or a cash-flow statement projecting a year-end loss are insufficient to meet Plaintiff's burden to show that defendant is likely to be "either insolvent or unable to pay money damages." (California Retail Portfolio Fund GMBH & Co. KG, 193 Cal.App.4th at 862.) Defendant's showing establishes that it is solvent, has met its debts and other financial obligations and has not defaulted on any such obligations, is financially sound, in good financial standing with its creditors, and has "millions of dollars" in its bank accounts. (Goodjohn Decl. 9.) Further, at no point has it declared or even considered declaring bankruptcy. (Id.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANNA GATTI VS. LOOP AI LAB INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 2, CROSS DEFENDANT ANNA GATTI MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Anna Gatti's motion for summary judgment on Loop AI Labs, Inc.'s amended cross-complaint is denied. As the court previously ruled, an order for the payment of money is considered a judgment for collection and enforcement purposes. (Code Civ. Proc. § 680.230 ["'Judgment' means a judgment, order, or decree entered in a court of this state."]; Code Civ. Proc. § 680.270 ["'Money judgment' means that part of a judgment that requires the payment of money."]; see Lucky United Properties Investment, Inc. v. Lee (2010) 185 Cal.App.4th 125, 139 fn.8 ["where costs (including attorney fees) are incorporated into a judgment of dismissal, the judgment becomes to that extent a money judgment"] [award of fees on anti-SLAPP motion]; Alioto Fish Co., Ltd. v. Alioto (1994) 27 Cal.App.4th 1669, 1686-1687 [order awarding fees is a judgment within the meaning of § 680.230].) Judge Gilliam's termination order did not clearly extinguish the prior fee orders. Loop's request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANNA GATTI VS. LOOP AI LAB INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Or Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT LOOP AI LABS INC., GIANMAURO CALAFIORE's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Or Summary Adjudication. Defendants Loop AI Labs, Inc. and Gianmauro Calafiore's motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied. Defendants' motion is procedurally and substantively deficient. Defendants' notice of motion and separate statement of undisputed facts does not comply with California Rule of Court 3.1350. Defendants do not separately state in the notice of motion or in the separate statement what issues or causes of action they seek to adjudicate. (See Cal. R. Ct. 3.1350(b),(h).) Defendants further fail to "plainly and concisely" set forth all material facts they contend are undisputed. (See Code of Civ. Proc., §437c(b)(1).) In addition, most of defendants' citations to supporting evidence are to multi-page exhibits with no pin cites. (UMF 2, 3, 10-19.) The court denies this motion because of the various defects. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(b)(1) ["The failure to comply with this requirement of a separate statement may in the court's discretion constitute a sufficient ground for denying the motion"]; Reeves v. Safeway Stores, Inc. (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 95, 106 [court's discretion to deny summary judgment based on defective separate statement]. In addition, much of the cited evidence is inadmissible or not probative. For example, defendants cannot rely on their own answer to maintain their initial burden of production. (See Hearn Pac. Corp. v. Second Generation Roofing, Inc. (2016) 247 Cal.App.4th 117, 131 [party may not offensively rely on own pleading on summary judgment]; UMF 3-5.) Defendants' citation to Gatti's objections to discovery similarly fails to prove anything. (UMF 14.) Such objections are not "factually devoid" discovery responses. (See Gaggero v. Yura (2003) 108 Cal.App.4th 884, 893 ["a deposition objection and instruction not to answer are not the equivalent of a factually devoid response."].) Moreover, Plaintiff points out that she served supplemental responses to those discovery requests, which Defendants fail to address. (Gatti Decl., Ex. 10.) rulings.Case Title: NATHAN ROSS VS. DAN GAILEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Attendance, Testimony, And Production Of Documents, To Deem Requests For Admission Admitted, And For Monetary Sanction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF NATHAN ROSS' Motion To Compel Attendance, Testimony, And Production Of Documents, To Deem Requests For Admission Admitted, And For Monetary Sanction. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion GRANTED. No timely opposition filed. Defendant Dan Gailey shall sit for deposition on October 17, 2019, at Plaintiff's counsel's office starting at 9:00 AM. The motion to deem admitted is granted. CCP 2033.280. Sanctions related to a motion under CCP 2033.280 are mandatory. Therefore, the request for sanctions is granted in the amount of $1,313.90, payable by October 17, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY HANSEN ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT PAUL YEP DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Paul Yep's demurrer to Plaintiff Timothy Hansen's first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. A viable claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress requires "outrageous conduct" on behalf of the defendant. (Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209.) For conduct to be outrageous, it must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Id.) "The tort calls for intentional, or at least reckless conduct - conduct intended to inflict injury or engaged in with the realization that injury will result." (Id. at 210.) "[I]t it generally held that there can be no recovery for mere profanity, obscenity, or abuse, without circumstances of aggravation, or for insults, indignities or threats which are considered to amount to nothing more than mere annoyances." (Yurick v. Superior Court (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1116, 1128; see also Kruse v. Bank of America (1988) 202 Cal.App.3d 38, 68 ["heartless and insensitive remarks [may] merit opprobrium, [but] they do not qualify as the kind of 'outrageous' conduct necessary to support an action for intentional infliction of emotional distress"].) Here, Defendant's conduct as pled does not constitute outrageous conduct as a matter of law. Defendant's alleged evasiveness, statement that he is a police officer while throwing down his identification, and driving past the Hansen household while staring in a "menacing fashion," are annoyances, but do not rise to the level of extreme and intolerable behavior. (1st Am. Compl. 5, 26-32.) Although an allegation of intoxication while driving a motor vehicle is regarded with dismay, the alleged intoxication is not outrageous and "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Davidson v. City of Westminster, 32 Cal.3d at 209.) Nor does Defendant's alleged conduct rise to the level of intimidation under the color of authority. (Cf., e.g., Tekle v. U.S. (9th Cir. 2007) 511 F.3d 839, 856 [outrageous conduct found where the officer kept their weapons pointed at the plaintiff, picked him off the ground by the handcuffs, made disparaging comments, and spit on him]; Crain v. Krehiel (N.D.Cal. 1977) 443 F.Supp. 202, 212 [agents' alleged threat to "burn" plaintiff in court could reasonably be construed as a threat to expose informant and was therefore considered outrageous].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY HANSEN ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT PAUL YEP Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint. Defendant Paul Yep's motion to strike punitive damages and malice allegations from Plaintiffs Timothy Hansen and Erin Hansen's complaint is denied. The allegations in the complaint support a prayer of punitive damages. Civil Code section 3294(a) limits punitive damages to circumstances "where it is proven by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice." Punitive damages may not be pled with conclusory allegations; the plaintiff must plead facts to support the request. (See G.D. Searle & Co. v. Superior Court (1975) 49 Cal.App.3d 22, 29 ["Notwithstanding relaxed pleading criteria, certain tortious injuries demand firm allegations. Vague, conclusory allegations . . . may not be rescued by the rule of liberal construction."].) As explained in the Court's ruling on Defendant's demurrer, there is no basis for a cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. However, the Court finds there are other specifically pled facts to support a finding of malice. The first amended complaint alleges Defendant smelled of alcohol, his speech was slurred, and he had an unsteady gait. (1st Am. Compl. 6, 12.) Furthermore, Defendant is a police officer and Plaintiff alleges Defendant was drinking before the accident occurred, knew the illegality of operating a motor vehicle while under the influence of alcohol, was aware of the danger, and acted with conscious disregard of this danger and the safety of others. (Id. 12-13.) "One who willfully consumes alcoholic beverages to the point of intoxication, knowing that he thereafter must operate a motor vehicle, thereby combining sharply impaired physical and mental faculties with a vehicle capable of great force and speed, reasonably may be held to exhibit a conscious disregard of the safety of others." (Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 897; see also, e.g., Dawes v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 82, 88 [Taylor held that "driving a vehicle while intoxicated may in appropriate circumstances evidence a conscious disregard of probable injury to others and be sufficient to warrant an award of punitive damages."] [holding that trial court improperly struck allegations of conduct by allegedly intoxicated driver that would support a recovery of punitive damages].) Plaintiffs sufficiently allege that Defendant was aware of the probable dangerous consequences of his conduct and that he deliberately failed to avoid those consequences. (See Taylor v. Superior Court, 24 Cal.3d at 856; Civ. Code, § 3294, subd. (c)(1).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiffs are required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KENNETH P STOLLER MD VS. DENNIS HERRERA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 7, 2019, Line 13, PLAINTIFF KENNETH STOLLER's Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Complaint. Plaintiff Kenneth P. Stoller, M.D.'s motion for leave to file a second amended complaint is granted. (See, e.g., Howard v. County of San Diego (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1422, 1428 ["The policy favoring amendment is so strong that it is a rare case in which denial of leave to amend can be justified."].) The parties shall meet and confer before Defendants file a demurrer to the second amended complaint. (Code Civ. Proc. § 430.41.) The validity of that proposed complaint, which differs very substantially from the existing first amended complaint, can then be addressed on the basis of a clear record. (See Brown v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 824, 843 ["The 'better course of action' is to allow a plaintiff to amend the complaint 'and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings.'"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
